message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The proportion of SARS-CoV-2 positive persons who are asymptomatic – and whether this proportion is age-dependent – are still open research questions. Because an unknown proportion of reported symptoms among SARS-CoV-2 positives will be attributable to another infection or affliction, the
                    <jats:italic>observed</jats:italic>
                    , or ‘crude’ proportion without symptoms may underestimate the proportion of persons without symptoms that are
                    <jats:italic>caused</jats:italic>
                    by SARS-CoV-2 infection.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Based on a large population-based serological study comprising test results on seropositivity and self-reported symptom history conducted in April/May 2020 in the Netherlands (
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =3147), we estimated the proportion of reported symptoms among those persons infected with SARS-CoV-2 that is attributable to this infection, where the set of relevant symptoms fulfills the ECDC case definition of COVID-19, using inferential methods for the attributable risk (AR). Generalised additive regression modelling was used to estimate the age-dependent relative risk (RR) of reported symptoms, and the AR and asymptomatic proportion (AP) were calculated from the fitted RR.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Using age-aggregated data, the estimated AP was 70% (95% CI: 65-77%). The estimated AP decreased with age, from 80% (95% CI: 67-100%) for the <20 years age-group, to 55% (95% CI: 48-68%) for the 70+ years age-group.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Whereas the ‘crude’ AP represents a lower bound for the proportion of persons infected with SARS-CoV-2 without COVID-19 symptoms, the AP as estimated via an attributable risk approach represents an upper bound. Age-specific AP estimates can inform the implementation of public health actions such as targetted virological testing and therefore enhance containment strategies.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Estimating the asymptomatic proportion of SARS-CoV-2 infection in the general population: Analysis of a nationwide serosurvey in the Netherlands

reference.unstructured: Meyerowitz EA , Richterman A , Bogoch II , Low N , Cevik M. Towards an accurate and systematic characterisation of persistently asymptomatic infection with SARS-CoV-2. Lancet Infect Dis. 2020.

Beale S , Hayward A , Shallcross L , Aldridge RW , Fragaszy E. Rapid review of the asymptomatic proportion of PCR-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infections in community settings. medRxiv. 2020. https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.05.20.20108183.
Goldstein E , Lipsitch M , Cevik M. On the effect of age on the transmission of SARS-CoV-2 in households, schools and the community. J Infect Dis. 2020
